segments.s604-g17: 'همان ماده' = 'that same female (animal)', with 'همان' a demonstrative pointing back to the previously mentioned creature and 'ماده' meaning a female animal. 'آهنگ ... کرد' is a light-verb construction: 'آهنگ' here = 'intention/aim' and with the past tense verb 'کرد' it means 'directed her aim toward / made for Bahram'. 'بهرام' is the proper name of the person aimed at.
segments.s604-g18: 'بغرید' is a simple past verb 'she roared/bellowed.' The coordinating 'و' links the next clause. 'چنگش' = 'her claw(s)' (possessive -ش = 'her/its') and 'به اندام کرد' is another light-verb phrase meaning 'laid (her claw) on / struck into the body' ('به' = toward/into, 'اندام' = body/limb, 'کرد' = did/made).
segments.s604-g19: 'یکی' = 'someone/a certain one' introduces an agent. 'تیغ زد' is the past tense 'struck (with a sword)' where 'تیغ' names the instrument and 'زد' is the verb 'struck'. 'بر میانش' = 'on/at his middle' ('بر' = on/against, 'میانش' = his middle/midriff). 'سوار' is the noun 'rider' and is the patient/target of the blow.
segments.s604-g20: 'فروماند' = 'sank down / was overcome / fell' (past result). 'جنگی' functions as an epithet 'warlike / the warrior' describing the rider. 'دران' = 'in that' (locative pointing back to the place of fighting) and 'کارزار' = 'battle' (the scene where he fell). The phrase orders the result (falling) followed by description and location.
segments.s604-g21: 'برون آمد' is the past verb 'came out / came forth.' 'از بیشه' is the source phrase 'from the grove/thicket.' 'مردی کهن' is an indefinite noun phrase 'an old man' (مردی = a man, کهن = old/ancient). The overall structure is a simple past-clause of movement.
segments.s604-g22: 'زبانش' = 'his tongue / his speech' (زبان + possessive -ش). 'گشاده' = 'open' used idiomatically = 'fluent' or 'free/ready' in speaking. 'به شیرین سخن' is a locative/expressive complement 'in sweet speech' (به ... سخن = 'in the manner of ... speech'), so the clause means his speech was fluent and sweet.
segments.s604-g23: The clause identifies the man's name. 'کجا' is a classical/poetic introductory particle here (literally 'where') used to introduce the identification that follows. 'نام او مهربنداد بود' = 'his name was Mehrbandad' with the past copula 'بود'.
segments.s604-g24: 'ازان' = 'from that / because of that' fronting the cause. 'زخم شمشیر' = 'the sword-wound' (زخم modified by شمشیر). 'او' = 'he' (subject pronoun). 'شاد بود' = past copula 'was glad' (شاد = happy, بود = was). The whole means 'he was glad because of that sword-wound.'
segments.s604-g9: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین adverb 'thus' before the verb). کاین is an archaic fused form of که این introducing a reported clause ('that this/that ...'); جای شیران بود is the reported proposition with جای 'place' as the noun and شیران (literally 'lions', here a poetic term for brave men) as genitive/possessive. بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause order is literary/poetic but parses as 'he said that this place was of lions.'
segments.s604-g10: همان رزمگاه دلیران بود = 'that was the battlefield of the brave.' همان is a demonstrative 'that' (or 'that very'); رزمگاه is a compound noun 'battlefield/place of battle'; دلیران is the plural of دلیر 'brave ones' functioning as genitive/possessive; بود is the past copula 'was'. Word order is nominal + copula (typical past descriptive).
segments.s604-g11: کمان را به زه کرد = 'he strung the bow.' کمان را uses the object marker را to mark the definite object 'the bow.' به زه کردن is a verbal phrase meaning 'to put the string to (the bow)' — here زه is 'bowstring' and کرد is the past verb 'did/made' (causative/lexical 'to string'). مرد دلیر follows as the agent/subject 'the brave man' (poetic inversion: subject after the verb).
segments.s604-g12: پدید آمد اندر زمان نره شیر = 'appeared / came forth, at that time a male lion.' پدید آمد is a compound verbal phrase 'appeared/came into sight.' اندر زمان is a temporal prepositional phrase 'in/at the time.' نره is an adjective 'male' modifying شیر 'lion' (noun follows), so the whole phrase names the appearing creature. Word order (verb then time then noun phrase) is poetic but grammatically clear.
segments.s604-g13: یکی نعره زد شیر = 'one (of them) the lion uttered a cry/roared.' یکی here marks 'one' or 'a certain one' (one lion), نعره زد is the idiom 'to utter a cry/roar' with زد as the past verb 'made/sounded.' چون در رسید = 'when/as it reached the gate,' where چون introduces a temporal clause and در here means 'gate.' The clause order is narrative and somewhat compressed as common in epic verse.
segments.s604-g14: بزد دست شاه و کمان درکشید = 'he struck with his hand, and (he) drew the bow.' In this poetic line the subject 'شاه' (the king) comes after the verb phrase; بزد (past of زدن) here means 'struck/struck (with his hand)' and دست functions as the instrument/object 'with his hand.' The coordinating و links the second action کمان درکشید, where درکشید ('drew in/ pulled') is the compound verb 'to draw (the bow).' Both actions share شاه as their agent by ellipsis of an explicit repeated subject.
segments.s604-g15: بزد تیر و پهلوش با دل بدوخت = 'He shot an arrow, and it pierced its flank and fastened into its heart.' بزد تیر is 'he struck/shot an arrow' (past tense); و links the next result clause. پهلوش is پهلو + -ش (its flank); با دل literally 'to/into the heart' here marks the target; بدوخت is the poetic past of دوختن 'to sew/fasten,' used metaphorically for 'to pierce or stick into' (the arrow lodging). The sequence describes the arrow's effect on the animal.
segments.s604-g16: دل شیر ماده بدوبر بسوخت = 'The she-lion's heart burned on both sides.' دل شیر ماده is a genitive construction 'the heart of the she-lion' (ماده = female animal). بدوبر is an adverbial compound 'on both sides / in two places,' and بسوخت is the past intransitive 'burned/was burned.' The whole clause reports the fatal wound's effect figuratively as burning of the heart on both sides.
segments.s604-g33: This is a nominal phrase where خداوند functions as 'owner/master' and the three animal nouns follow as his possessions. Poetic prose often omits the ezāfe (ِ) or a possessive marker, so the sequence is read as “the master/owner of cows, donkeys, and sheep.” Word order is head noun then listed possessed items.
segments.s604-g34: ز here means 'because of / from' and introduces the cause phrase ز شیران. شده is the past participle/compound meaning 'has become' (linking to the following predicate adjectives). بددل و مستمند are predicate adjectives describing the subject introduced earlier. The whole segment expresses a resultant state caused by the lions.
segments.s604-g35: کنون is an adverb 'now'. ایزد is an invocation of God/Providence as the agent (subject) of the wish. این کار is the direct object 'this affair.' بر دست تو is a prepositional phrase ('onto/into your hand') marking the destination or locus of the action; the verb appears later, so word order follows classical Persian adjacency (adverb/subject/object/place → verb).
segments.s604-g36: برآورد here is used optatively/jussively (a wish or prayer): 'may (he) bring/put.' The following بر plus قبضه و شست تو repeats the directional phrase—'into your grip and your thumb'—with قبضه 'grip/handle' and شست 'thumb' specifying parts of the hand (idiomatically meaning firm control). The verb is thus a third‑person optative/polite form used in older poetic Persian.
segments.s604-g37: زمانی functions as a temporal/conditional adverb 'if/when ever' (introducing a contingent clause). درین is the poetic compound of در + این ('in this'), and بیشه is 'grove.' آیی is the second‑person singular present/subjunctive of 'to come' ('you come' / 'should you come'). چنان/چنین at the end qualifies the manner: 'thus/so.' Word order places the conditional/time phrase and place before the verb.
segments.s604-g38: بباشی is the second‑person singular subjunctive/imperative of 'to be' meaning 'be / remain (so)'. به introduces accompaniment or sphere ('among/with'), followed by a triad of nouns شیر (here 'lions'), می‌و (poetic 'fruit'), and انگبین ('nectar' or ambrosial honey). The phrase prescribes how the addressee should comport themselves if they enter the grove.
segments.s604-g39: بره هست is an existential clause 'there is a lamb' (subject + copula/existential هست). چندانک is a connective adverb 'to that extent/so that,' linking the existence to the consequence introduced by باید ('it must'). باید is the modal 'must/ought to,' and به کار here means 'to (be) used for work'—the infinitival/complimentary phrase following باید.
segments.s604-g40: درختان is the plural head noun 'trees.' It is modified by the two adjectives بارآور ('fruitful, productive') and سایه‌دار ('shade‑giving / shaded'), joined by و 'and.' Together they form the goal/beneficiaries of the action expressed earlier (to be used for the work of these trees).
segments.s604-g1: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning "when." یوز شکاری is a noun plus postpositive adjective (cheetah/hunting), and به کار آمدش is an idiomatic past construction: به کار آمد means "came into use/into action," with آمدش = آمد + enclitic -ش referring back to the animal ("it/ him").
segments.s604-g2: بجنبید is a past-form verb (in epic/classical style with the preverb ب-) meaning "it moved/was roused." و links the clauses. رای شکار آمدش is a small clause where رای ("plan/instinct/idea") is the thing that "came" (آمدش) to the animal; آمدش again carries the enclitic -ش referring to the same referent.
segments.s604-g3: یکی باره‌ای functions as an adverbial phrase meaning "one time/once" or "suddenly/a certain time." تیزرو is an epithet "swift (runner)." بر نشست is a past action: بر + نشست = "alighted upon/settled on," with بر marking contact or direction.
segments.s604-g4: به هامون gives directionality with به "to/toward" + هامون "the plain." خرامید is the past verb "he strode/proudly walked." بازی به دست is an instrumental/manner phrase: بازی "play/playing" and به دست "with (in) the hand/paw," where به introduces the manner/instrument ("with").
segments.s604-g5: یکی بیشه is an indefinite noun phrase "a certain thicket/grove." پیش آمدش is a compound where پیش ("before/in front") + آمدش (past with enclitic -ش) means "came up/appeared before him/it;" پردرخت is a postpositive adjective "leafy/wooded" modifying بیشه.
segments.s604-g6: نشستنگه is a compound noun meaning "seat/plac e to sit" (a resting place). مردم نیک‌بخت is a noun phrase in apposition/genitive relation modifying نشستنگه: "the people (مردم) who are fortunate (نیک‌بخت)," i.e. "a resting-place of fortunate people."
segments.s604-g7: بسان بهشتی is a simile "like/paradise-like" (بسان = "like/as" and بهشتی = "heavenly/paradise-like"). یکی سبز جای is a noun phrase "a single/one green place" (یکی as "one/a", سبز "green", جای "place").
segments.s604-g8: ندید is the past verb with the negative prefix ن- (classical Persian uses ندید to mean "did not see" here); اندرو = اندر + او/it = "in/within it." مردم و چارپای lists the two groups "people and quadrupeds/beasts" that did not see such a place. Word order places the negated verb before the locative and the subjects.
segments.s604-g25: This is a nominal phrase introducing the character. یکی here means 'a certain' (not the numeral); مرد = 'man/person' and دهقان specifies his social role 'peasant/farmer'. The postpositive adjective یزدان‌پرست ('God‑worshipper') qualifies the whole noun phrase.
segments.s604-g26: بدان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in/at that' formed from ب(ـه) + دان (here functioning as demonstrative 'that'); بیشه = 'grove/thicket'. بودیش is a possessive/attributive past form meaning 'belonged to him' or 'was his', and جای نشست means 'place (of) settlement' — together giving 'his dwelling/place was in that grove'.
segments.s604-g27: چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when he came'. بر + شاه ایران indicates motion/toward or presence 'before the King of Iran'; فراز is a poetic adverbial 'on high/aloft', placed after the phrase for metrical/poetic effect.
segments.s604-g28: This sequence describes his actions on approaching: برو is used as a verbal adverb or perfective 'he went up/approached', آفرین کرد = 'he uttered praise/exclamation ("afarin")' with کرد as the past auxiliary 'did/uttered', and بردش نماز describes an action involving نماز ('prayer'). Grammatically بادِش/بردش is an old/clipped verbal form meaning 'he took/led/performed for him' (context determines exact nuance); overall: 'he approached, cried "afarin", and performed/took prayer.'
segments.s604-g29: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him'). کای is a vocative 'O', here addressing the ruler; مهتر نامدار is the vocative noun phrase 'renowned/chiefly elder' (مهتر = chief/distinguished man, نامدار = renowned).
segments.s604-g30: This is an optative wish: به کام تو = 'to/for your desire' (i.e. 'in your favor'); باد is the optative particle 'may' (archaic/poetic); اختر روزگار = 'the star of fate/times' (genitive phrase). Word order is inverted for poetic effect: literally 'to your desire may (be) the star of fate.'
segments.s604-g31: یکی مرد دهقانم begins a self‑identification. یکی = 'a certain', مرد = 'man', and دهقانم is دهقان + the 1st‑person copular/enclitic -م meaning 'I am a peasant' (an older/poetic way to say 'I am a peasant'). ای is the vocative 'O', and پاک‑رای is an epithet 'of pure reputation/virtuous', addressing the addressee.
segments.s604-g32: This phrase begins to enumerate what belongs to him or what he speaks of: خداوند here is an honorific 'lord/sovereign' used to address the king (not literally 'God'); این جا = 'this place'; و کشت = 'and the cultivated land/fields' (کشت = cultivation/fields); و سرای would be 'and the house/homestead' but the provided lemma senses for سرای do not include that meaning in the list, so it functions here as 'house/household'. The phrase is a nominal list (likely continued in following lines).
segments.s604-g57: چنان is an adverb meaning 'so' or 'in such a way' placed before the verb. آفریند is the archaic/poetic 3rd-person present (or optative) form of آفریدن 'to create/make' meaning 'he makes/fashions'. که introduces the following subordinate clause ('that'); خواهد is the classical auxiliary showing volition/future 'he wills' and همی is a classical imperfective/emphatic particle, here adding continuity/emphasis to خواهد.
segments.s604-g58: مر is the old dative/benefactive clitic 'to/for me' placed before the object. آن را is the definite object ('that' + object marker را). گزیند is the classical 3rd-person present/optative of گزینیدن 'choose' ('he chooses/shall choose'). که introduces the relative clause, and خواهد همی repeats the volitive/future auxiliary plus the continuous/emphatic particle — 'which he desires/wills.'
segments.s604-g59: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. من is the subject 'I'. همی is the classical continuous/ emphatic particle (here marking ongoing state). نیک is a predicative adjective/adverb 'well/good'. مانم is the 1st-person present subjunctive/indicative of ماندن 'to remain/be (regarded as)' — 'I remain' or 'I continue to be' — and به شاه ('to/with respect to the king') marks the beneficiary/target (i.e. 'in the king's sight').
segments.s604-g60: ت (here written separately) is the clitic for the 2nd-person object (تو → ترا) and together with را (the definite object marker) yields 'to you' as the recipient. دادم is the 1st-person past 'I gave', here functioning as a promise/offer ('I will give' in sense). این بیشه و جایگاه is the direct object 'this grove and estate/place.' Note that classical poetic usage sometimes uses the past tense to express a forthcoming gift or settled promise.
segments.s604-g61: بگفت is the past narrative form 'he said'. این 'this' is the object/quote of what was said. و coordinates the clauses ('and'). زان جایگه = 'from that place' (زان = 'from that'), and برنشست is a past verb 'he rose up / mounted / left (the place)' — in poetry verb-first or subject/instrument-order variations are common.
segments.s604-g62: به introduces the direction 'to' (toward). ایوان is the destination 'iwan/porch/veranda'. خرم is an adjective 'pleasant/joyful/lush' modifying the iwan. خرامید is the past form 'he strode/sashayed' (a proud, measured gait). مست is an adjective/adverb 'drunk' or 'intoxicated', here characterizing the manner of his striding ('strode, intoxicated').
segments.s604-g63: بخفت is the past narrative verb 'he slept' (verb-first order is common in poetry). آن شب تیره is a time phrase 'that dark night' (تیره = dark/bleak). در بوستان is the locative phrase 'in the garden.' Together they specify when and where the sleeping occurred.
segments.s604-g64: همی is the imperfective/continuous particle 'kept/continued to' marking repeated action. یاد is the noun 'remembrance' which with the light verb کرد ('did/made') forms the compound 'یاد کردن' = 'to remember/recollect' (کرد is the past tense 'he did/remembered'). از لب دوستان is a prepositional phrase 'from the lips of friends' (از + لب = 'from the words/speech of'), i.e. 'he recalled the words said by his friends.'
segments.s604-g49: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning 'when/after.' نان خورده شد is a perfect/passive-style clause ('bread was eaten' / 'they had eaten bread') with شد as a past auxiliary; the following noun phrase جامهای نبید (cups of wine) is left without a verb here and will be linked to the main verb in the next segment.
segments.s604-g50: نهادند is a past plural verb 'they placed/set'; پیشش ('before him/in his presence') is the locative adverbial; گل و شنبلید are the coordinated direct objects ('flowers and fenugreek/flowering herb') that were placed before him. Subject is omitted and recovered from context ('they').
segments.s604-g51: چو شد begins another temporal clause 'when ... became'; مهربنداد is the personal name (subject) and شادان is a predicate adjective 'merry/joyful'; ز می ('from wine') gives the cause. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (verb before name).
segments.s604-g52: به بهرام گفت is 'he said to Bahram' (به marks the indirect object). The following words form direct speech: ای is the vocative 'O!', گو is an imperative 'say/speak', and نیک‌پی is a vocative epithet 'well‑born' (addressing Bahram). Note that گفت is the speech verb introducing the quotation.
segments.s604-g53: چنان دان is an imperative phrase 'consider thus/think so' (چنان = 'thus/so', دان = 'know/consider'). که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...'; ماننده‌ای is 'being like/one like', and شاه را uses the object/definiteness marker را to highlight 'a king' as the standard of comparison.
segments.s604-g54: همان is an emphatic determiner 'that very/the same'; تخت زرین is a noun phrase 'golden throne'; و links the second item; هم‌گاه here appears as a parallel definite noun (poetically 'the same seat/occasion') and را again marks the whole as definite objects of the preceding consideration.
segments.s604-g55: بدو گفت بهرام is a verb‑first poetic order meaning 'Bahram said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said', بهرام = subject). کآری ('this deed/that action') is the topic and رواست is the predicate 'is fitting/permissible' (روا + است).
segments.s604-g56: نگارنده is the noun 'painter/inscriber' (subject of the predication in the quoted clause); بر is the preposition 'on/upon'; چهرها is the plural 'faces' (object of بر); پادشاست is a nominal predicate meaning 'is king/sovereign' (poetic claim that the painter rules over faces). Together the phrase asserts the painter's supremacy over facial depiction.
segments.s604-g41: فرود آمد is a past tense verbal phrase meaning 'alighted/descended' (فرود = alighting + آمد = came). The following prepositional phrase از باره literally 'from the bārah' uses باره in a poetic/archaic sense 'litter, load, baggage' (i.e. vehicle), and بهرامشاه, the proper name, follows the phrase as the logical subject — an inversion common in epic Persian (the subject can come after the verb or the prepositional phrase for emphasis).
segments.s604-g42: همی کرد is an older/perhaps poetic imperfective construction: همی before a past-tense verb gives a continuous or iterative nuance ('kept doing' / 'was doing'). کرد is the past of 'to do' and here takes نگاه ('a look, gaze') as its object; زان بیشه جایی is a locative phrase 'for that/the grove a place' (زان = 'that/from that', بیشه = 'grove', جایی = 'a place/somewhere'). Word order places the verb phrase before the locative phrase for poetic flow.
segments.s604-g43: که introduces a subordinate (relative/locative) clause 'where/that', and باشد here is the literary present/subjunctive copula meaning 'is/ be' (used to describe the desired character of the place). زمین سبز ('ground green') and آب روان ('water flowing') are noun+adjective phrases in the normal Persian noun–adjective order.
segments.s604-g44: چنانچون is a comparative/simile 'just as/like', and بود is the past copula 'was' linking to جای مرد جوان ('the place of the young man'). Word order is canonical: the simile particle begins the clause, followed by the past copula and then the noun phrase functioning as the standard of comparison.
segments.s604-g45: بشد is an archaic/narrative particle meaning 'it came to pass' / 'then' (used to advance the story). مهربنداد is a personal name; و joins it to رامشگران, a plural noun meaning 'peacemakers/comforters' (here people who calm or arrange). The clause functions as a narrative clause introducing these characters.
segments.s604-g46: بیاورد is a past/simple narrative verb 'brought' (here agreeing with the previously introduced agents). چندی is a quantifier 'some/several', ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and مهتران here means 'the chief/elder men' (i.e. village notables); the phrase ز ده مهتران (the text has 'ده' = village implied elsewhere) indicates they brought some elders from the village. The verb precedes its object phrase due to the poetic line rhythm.
segments.s604-g47: بسی is an older quantifier meaning 'many/a great number' (here modifying گوسفندان). گوسفندان فربه is a noun phrase 'fat sheep' (plural), and بکشت is an archaic past verb form meaning 'killed/slaughtered' (the prefix بـ appears in older Persian verb forms). Typical epic compression places the quantifier before the noun and the verb at line end.
segments.s604-g48: بیامد is a narrative past 'came/approached'. یکی جام زرین is a noun phrase 'a golden cup' (یکی = 'one/a certain', جام = 'cup', زرین = 'golden'). به مشت is a locative/ablative phrase 'into/into the fist' or 'in the fist' (به + مشت indicating direction or manner — 'with/in his fist'). Together the clause describes someone coming forward holding a golden cup.

sentences.s604-s9: That same she-beast made for Bahram, roared, and drove her claws into his body.
sentences.s604-s10: Someone struck the rider in the middle with a sword; the warlike rider sank down in that battle.
sentences.s604-s11: An old man came out of the grove; his tongue was open to sweet speech (he spoke sweetly).
sentences.s604-s12: His name was Mehrbandad; he was glad because of that sword-wound.
sentences.s604-s5: Thus he said, 'This was the place of lions; that was the battlefield of the brave.'
sentences.s604-s6: The brave man strung the bow, and at that time a male lion appeared.
sentences.s604-s7: A lion gave a roar; when it reached the gate, the king struck with his hand and drew his bow.
sentences.s604-s8: He shot an arrow, and it pierced the flank and lodged in the heart; the she-lion's heart burned on both sides.
sentences.s604-s17: The owner of cows, donkeys, and sheep has become faint‑hearted and impoverished because of the lions.
sentences.s604-s18: May God now put this matter into your hand—into your grip and your thumb.
sentences.s604-s19: If ever you come into this grove, be so—among lions and fruit and nectar.
sentences.s604-s20: There is a lamb; therefore it must be put to work for the fruitful and shade‑giving trees.
sentences.s604-s1: When the hunting cheetah came into action, it stirred, and the urge/plan to hunt came upon it.
sentences.s604-s2: Suddenly a swift one alighted; he strode onto the plain, playing with it in his paw.
sentences.s604-s3: A leafy thicket rose up before him — a sitting‑place of fortunate people.
sentences.s604-s4: Like paradise: a single green place — neither men nor four‑footed beasts had seen such within it.
sentences.s604-s13: A certain peasant, a God‑worshipper, had his dwelling in that grove.
sentences.s604-s14: When he came before the King of Iran on high, he approached, cried 'bravo', and offered (or performed) prayer.
sentences.s604-s15: He said to him, 'O famed chief, may the star of the times be to your desire.'
sentences.s604-s16: I am a certain peasant, O virtuous one; this place and its fields and homestead...
sentences.s604-s29: He fashioned it so that, as he wishes, he may choose for me that which he wills.
sentences.s604-s30: If I remain in the king's good graces, I will give you this grove and estate.
sentences.s604-s31: He said this and from that place rose up; to the pleasant iwan he strode, intoxicated.
sentences.s604-s32: That dark night he slept in the garden; he kept remembering the words of his friends.
sentences.s604-s25: When the bread had been eaten, they set cups of wine before him and placed flowers and fenugreek.
sentences.s604-s26: When Mehrbandad became merry from wine, he said to Bahram, 'O well-born, speak!'
sentences.s604-s27: Consider it thus, that as if a king he has that very golden throne and the same seat.
sentences.s604-s28: To him Bahram said, 'That deed is fitting; the painter on faces is a sovereign.'
sentences.s604-s21: Bahramshah alighted from his litter and kept gazing for a spot in that grove.
sentences.s604-s22: — a place where the ground is green and the water running, just as the young man's place was.
sentences.s604-s23: Then Mehrbandād and the peacemakers came, and they brought some of the village elders.
sentences.s604-s24: They slaughtered many fat sheep; then one man came with a golden cup in his fist.
